_comment: Canonical Sanskrit + verbatim translations + anvaya + literal/traditional meanings for BG 6.1-6.47, used to assemble source packs and to validate verse records. Sanskrit text is public-domain Mahabharata Bhishma Parva text, cross-confirmed against vedabase.io / holy-bhagavad-gita.org / gitasupersite.iitk.ac.in / bhagavad-gita.us / GRETIL critical edition Bhg_06.0NN.

1.dev:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
अनाश्रितः कर्मफलं कार्यं कर्म करोति यः ।
स सन्न्यासी च योगी च न निरग्निर्न चाक्रियः ॥ १ ॥
1.iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
anāśritaḥ karma-phalaṁ kāryaṁ karma karoti yaḥ |
sa sannyāsī ca yogī ca na niragnir na cākriyaḥ || 1 ||
1.literal: The Blessed Lord said: One who performs prescribed work without depending on the fruits of action — that one is the renouncer (sannyāsī) and the yogi, not the one without ritual fire and not the one who avoids action.
1.traditional: Krishna opens chapter 6 by collapsing the apparent distinction between the sannyāsī (renouncer) and the yogī. What renunciation actually consists in is renunciation of dependence on the fruit of action, not renunciation of action itself. The merely fireless or merely actionless person is not by that fact a sannyāsī. Both Shankara and Ramanuja read this as Krishna's continuation of the chapter-5 equation: the karma-yogī performing duty without fruit-attachment IS the renouncer.
1.prabhupada: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: One who is unattached to the fruits of his work and who works as he is obligated is in the renounced order of life, and he is the true mystic, not he who lights no fire and performs no duty.
1.shankara: 6.1 Yah, whoever; karoti, performs; karma, action; karyam, that ought to be done, that is obligatory; anasritah, without depending on; karma-phalam, the result of action — saying, 'Let it produce its result; what use is it to me?' — sah, that one; is sannyasi, a monk; ca, and; yogi, a yogi engaged in karma-yoga.
1.ramanuja: 6.1 He who, without seeking the fruits of work, does works that ought to be done, i.e., the obligatory and occasional acts (Nitya and Naimittika Karmas), is a Sannyasin and Yogin both at once. Such a one is not without the sacred fire and not actionless.

2.dev:
यं सन्न्यासमिति प्राहुर्योगं तं विद्धि पाण्डव ।
न ह्यसन्न्यस्तसङ्कल्पो योगी भवति कश्चन ॥ २ ॥
2.iast:
yaṁ sannyāsam iti prāhur yogaṁ taṁ viddhi pāṇḍava |
na hy asannyasta-saṅkalpo yogī bhavati kaścana || 2 ||
2.literal: What they call sannyāsa, know that to be yoga, O Pāṇḍava — for no one becomes a yogi without renouncing fancies (saṅkalpa).
2.traditional: Krishna sharpens the equation. What is called renunciation IS what is called yoga; the two are one teaching. The cut is at saṅkalpa — the desire-laden imaginings about results — not at action itself. Without giving up these saṅkalpa-fancies no one becomes a yogi. The doctrine clarifies that what 6.1 collapsed was not the difference between two doctrinal labels but a real identity-claim about the inner orientation.
2.prabhupada: What is called renunciation you should know to be the same as yoga, or linking oneself with the Supreme, O son of Pāṇḍu, for one can never become a yogī unless he renounces the desire for sense gratification.
2.shankara: 6.2 What they speak of as Sannyasa, that, know, O Pandava, to be Yoga; for none becomes a Yogin without renouncing desires (sankalpa). Sankalpas — the imaginings born of desire about ends — are at the root of all karmas; their renunciation is the renunciation of all karmas in their root.
2.ramanuja: 6.2 Yoga and Sannyasa are not different. The renunciation of fruits of action is karma-yoga; that very renunciation, when extended to the saṅkalpa or motivating intention itself, is sannyāsa. No one truly becomes a yogi without renouncing the saṅkalpas.

3.dev:
आरुरुक्षोर्मुनेर्योगं कर्म कारणमुच्यते ।
योगारूढस्य तस्यैव शमः कारणमुच्यते ॥ ३ ॥
3.iast:
ārurukṣor muner yogaṁ karma kāraṇam ucyate |
yogārūḍhasya tasyaiva śamaḥ kāraṇam ucyate || 3 ||
3.literal: For the muni desiring to ascend yoga, action (karma) is said to be the means; for that same one ascended in yoga, calm (śama) is said to be the means.
3.traditional: Krishna distinguishes two stages of the practitioner. The aspirant climbing toward yoga has action as the means of ascent. The same person, once established in yoga, has calm — cessation of agitation — as the operative means. The verse is structural: it explains why the chapter will move shortly from action-instruction to meditation-instruction. Shankara emphasises the staged reading; Ramanuja reads it as a continuum within karma-yoga itself.
3.prabhupada: For one who is a neophyte in the eightfold yoga system, work is said to be the means; and for one who is already elevated in yoga, cessation of all material activities is said to be the means.
3.shankara: 6.3 Of the man of meditation who is desirous of ascending, who has not attained to steadiness in yoga, karma is said to be the means; but of the same man when he has ascended yoga, sama, withdrawal from all karmas, is said to be the means.
3.ramanuja: 6.3 For one who is yet to ascend in yoga, action without attachment is the means; for one who has ascended, the calm of the yoked mind, free from agitation, is the means by which higher yoga proceeds.

4.dev:
यदा हि नेन्द्रियार्थेषु न कर्मस्वनुषज्जते ।
सर्वसङ्कल्पसन्न्यासी योगारूढस्तदोच्यते ॥ ४ ॥
4.iast:
yadā hi nendriyārtheṣu na karmasv anuṣajjate |
sarva-saṅkalpa-sannyāsī yogārūḍhas tadocyate || 4 ||
4.literal: When one is no longer attached to sense-objects nor to actions — having renounced all fancies — one is said to be ascended in yoga.
4.traditional: Krishna defines the diagnostic of yogārūḍha (ascended in yoga). The marks: detachment from sense-objects, detachment from actions themselves, renunciation of all saṅkalpas. The verse closes the four-verse opening definition: 6.1 redefined renunciation; 6.2 anchored the cut at saṅkalpa; 6.3 staged the practice; 6.4 names the destination. The internal sequence is doctrinally complete.
4.prabhupada: A person is said to be elevated in yoga when, having renounced all material desires, he neither acts for sense gratification nor engages in fruitive activities.
4.shankara: 6.4 When indeed not in the objects of the senses nor in actions one is attached, then, having renounced all motives (sankalpas), the cause of all karmas, one is said to be elevated in yoga.
4.ramanuja: 6.4 He becomes 'ascended in yoga' when there is no attachment to objects of the senses (sound etc.) and no attachment to actions performed for the sake of those objects. Having renounced every motive (saṅkalpa) — both the desires and the imaginings that produce them — that one is yogārūḍha.
anvaya: in the objects of the senses

5.dev:
उद्धरेदात्मनात्मानं नात्मानमवसादयेत् ।
आत्मैव ह्यात्मनो बन्धुरात्मैव रिपुरात्मनः ॥ ५ ॥
5.iast:
uddhared ātmanātmānaṁ nātmānam avasādayet |
ātmaiva hy ātmano bandhur ātmaiva ripur ātmanaḥ || 5 ||
5.literal: Let one lift the self by the self; let one not let the self sink. The self alone is the friend of the self, and the self alone is the enemy of the self.
5.traditional: The most-quoted verse of the chapter. Krishna's central injunction: the self is the only lifter of the self. No external agent can do this work. The same self is both the friend and the enemy of the self — depending on whether it has been disciplined. Shankara reads ātman here as the embodied self, not the supreme Self; Ramanuja reads it the same way at this stage. The verse is operational, not metaphysical: the practitioner is the only one who can lift the practitioner.
5.prabhupada: One must deliver himself with the help of his mind, and not degrade himself. The mind is the friend of the conditioned soul, and his enemy as well.
5.shankara: 6.5 Let one lift up oneself, raise oneself out of the ocean of the world, by oneself — by the mind dragged towards aspiration; let one not let oneself sink. For oneself alone is the friend of oneself; there is no other friend that can lift one out of saṁsāra; oneself alone is the enemy of oneself if engaged in the unrighteous.
5.ramanuja: 6.5 One should lift up oneself by oneself; one should not depress oneself. For oneself alone is one's friend, and oneself alone is one's enemy. The mind, when it engages with sense-objects, is the cause of bondage; the same mind, when it ceases such engagement, is the cause of liberation.

6.dev:
बन्धुरात्मात्मनस्तस्य येनात्मैवात्मना जितः ।
अनात्मनस्तु शत्रुत्वे वर्तेतात्मैव शत्रुवत् ॥ ६ ॥
6.iast:
bandhur ātmātmanas tasya yenātmaivātmanā jitaḥ |
anātmanas tu śatrutve vartetātmaiva śatruvat || 6 ||
6.literal: For one by whom the self has been conquered by the self, the self is the friend of the self. But for one whose self is unconquered, the self itself acts as enemy, hostile-like.
6.traditional: Krishna names the bidirectional doctrine. The conquered self is the friend; the unconquered self is the enemy. The verse is sharp on both sides — the unconquered self is not just unhelpful, it acts in active enmity (śatru-vat, like an enemy). This is the verse the engineering analog must preserve in both directions: years of disciplined practice produce a self-system that supports the work; years of fragmentation produce a self-system that obstructs the work.
6.prabhupada: For him who has conquered the mind, the mind is the best of friends; but for one who has failed to do so, his mind will remain the greatest enemy.
6.shankara: 6.6 For him by whom the very self has been conquered by the very self, of him the self is the friend; but for one whose self is unconquered, the self itself remains in a state of enmity, like an external foe.
6.ramanuja: 6.6 The mind that has been subdued by the higher discriminating self is the friend of that very self; but the mind that has not been subdued behaves as the enemy of the self, opposing every attempt at concentration.
anvaya: of one whose self is not conquered

7.dev:
जितात्मनः प्रशान्तस्य परमात्मा समाहितः ।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु तथा मानापमानयोः ॥ ७ ॥
7.iast:
jitātmanaḥ praśāntasya paramātmā samāhitaḥ |
śītoṣṇa-sukha-duḥkheṣu tathā mānāpamānayoḥ || 7 ||
7.literal: For one whose self is conquered, who is fully calmed, the supreme self is concentrated — in cold and heat, pleasure and pain, honor and dishonor.
7.traditional: Krishna gives the equanimity-diagnostic. The conquered, calmed self has the supreme self concentrated within it; the diagnostic of this state is steadiness across the three pairs (cold/heat, pleasure/pain, honor/dishonor). The state is not 'doesn't feel' but 'doesn't disturb the concentrated state.' Shankara reads paramātmā as the supreme Self that becomes manifest as fully concentrated in such a person. Ramanuja reads it as the higher discriminating self, samāhita with concentration on the divine.
7.prabhupada: For one who has conquered the mind, the Supersoul is already reached, for he has attained tranquility. To such a man happiness and distress, heat and cold, honor and dishonor are all the same.
7.shankara: 6.7 The supreme Self is fully manifested, in samadhi as it were, in the man whose self has been conquered, who is calm — in heat and cold, in pleasure and pain, in honor and dishonor — being undisturbed.
7.ramanuja: 6.7 The mind of one whose self is subdued, who is at peace, becomes wholly composed in the divine Self, when he is in heat or cold, pleasure or pain, honour or disgrace alike.
anvaya: of one whose self is conquered

8.dev:
ज्ञानविज्ञानतृप्तात्मा कूटस्थो विजितेन्द्रियः ।
युक्त इत्युच्यते योगी समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः ॥ ८ ॥
8.iast:
jñāna-vijñāna-tṛptātmā kūṭastho vijitendriyaḥ |
yukta ity ucyate yogī sama-loṣṭāśma-kāñcanaḥ || 8 ||
8.literal: The yogi whose self is satisfied with knowledge and discriminative knowledge, who is unmoving as a peak, who has fully conquered the senses, who regards a clod, a stone, and gold as equal — that one is said to be yoked (yukta).
8.traditional: Krishna gives the second equanimity-diagnostic. The yukta-yogi: satisfied with knowledge (jñāna, theoretical) and discriminative knowledge (vijñāna, experiential), unmoving as a peak (kūṭastha), senses conquered, equal toward clod-stone-gold. The threefold equality (loṣṭa-aśma-kāñcana) is the practical test of detachment from material distinctions.
8.prabhupada: A person is said to be established in self-realization and is called a yogī when he is fully satisfied by virtue of acquired knowledge and realization. Such a person is situated in transcendence and is self-controlled. He sees everything — whether it be pebbles, stones or gold — as the same.
8.shankara: 6.8 The yogi whose mind is satisfied with knowledge of the scriptures and personal experience, who remains immovable, who has subdued the senses, is said to be 'yukta' — established in steadfast yoga. To him, a clod of earth, a stone, and gold are alike.
8.ramanuja: 6.8 The Yogin who is satiated by Self-knowledge and Self-realization, who is unwavering, with the senses conquered, who looks alike on a lump of clay, a stone or gold, is said to be 'yukta' — fit for yoga.
anvaya: whose self is satisfied with knowledge and realization

9.iast:
suhṛn-mitrāry-udāsīna-madhyastha-dveṣya-bandhuṣu |
sādhuṣv api ca pāpeṣu sama-buddhir viśiṣyate || 9 ||
9.literal: One who has equal intellect toward well-wishers, friends, enemies, the indifferent, the mediator, the hated, kinsmen, and even toward the righteous and the sinful — that one excels.
9.traditional: Krishna extends the equanimity diagnostic from natural objects (clod-stone-gold) to relational categories (eight classes of persons + righteous/sinful). The verse names eight relational categories — well-wisher, friend, enemy, indifferent, mediator, hated, kinsman, plus saint and sinner — and claims the yogi who maintains equal intellect across all of them excels. Note: this is not endorsing moral indifference; both Shankara and Ramanuja read it as steadiness of the discriminating intellect across the relational pulls, not as absence of right-action.
9.prabhupada: A person is considered still further advanced when he regards honest well-wishers, affectionate benefactors, the neutral, mediators, the envious, friends and enemies, the pious and the sinners all with an equal mind.
9.shankara: 6.9 He is highly esteemed who has equal regard towards a benefactor, friend, foe, indifferent person, neutral, hateful, relative, and even towards the righteous and the unrighteous.
9.ramanuja: 6.9 He is the highest who is equal-minded toward suhṛd (well-wisher), mitra (friend), ari (enemy), udāsīna (the unconcerned), madhyastha (mediator), dveṣya (the hated), bandhu (kinsman), the saintly and the sinful — equal in his evaluation of his own state, undisturbed by the variations of their relation to him.
anvaya: toward well-wisher, friend, enemy, indifferent, mediator, hateful, kinsman

10.dev:
योगी युञ्जीत सततमात्मानं रहसि स्थितः ।
एकाकी यतचित्तात्मा निराशीरपरिग्रहः ॥ १० ॥
10.iast:
yogī yuñjīta satatam ātmānaṁ rahasi sthitaḥ |
ekākī yata-cittātmā nirāśīr aparigrahaḥ || 10 ||
10.literal: Let the yogi constantly yoke the self, dwelling in solitude, alone, with mind and body controlled, free of expectations, free of possessions.
10.traditional: Krishna pivots from disposition to practice. The yogi is to constantly yoke the self — in solitude, alone, with controlled mind and body, without expectations, without possessions. The verse opens the meditation-practice block (6.10-6.32). The four conditions — solitude, aloneness, mind-body control, freedom from expectation/possession — are preconditions for the somatic instruction that follows.
10.prabhupada: A transcendentalist should always engage his body, mind and self in relationship with the Supreme; he should live alone in a secluded place and should always carefully control his mind. He should be free from desires and feelings of possessiveness.
10.shankara: 6.10 Let the yogi constantly concentrate his mind by remaining in seclusion, alone, with body and mind controlled, free from desires, and without possessions.
10.ramanuja: 6.10 The Yogin should constantly fix his mind on the Self, dwelling alone in some secluded place, with mind and body controlled, free from longings and from the sense of mineness over possessions.

11.dev:
शुचौ देशे प्रतिष्ठाप्य स्थिरमासनमात्मनः ।
नात्युच्छ्रितं नातिनीचं चैलाजिनकुशोत्तरम् ॥ ११ ॥
11.iast:
śucau deśe pratiṣṭhāpya sthiram āsanam ātmanaḥ |
nāty-ucchritaṁ nāti-nīcaṁ cailājina-kuśottaram || 11 ||
11.literal: In a clean place, having established a firm seat for oneself, neither too high nor too low, with cloth-deer-skin-kuśa-grass on top.
11.traditional: Krishna's first piece of somatic instruction: the seat. The conditions are concrete — clean place, firm seat, of moderate height (neither high nor low), prepared with cloth, deer-skin, and kuśa-grass on top. Shankara and Ramanuja both read this literally; this is yogic instruction in the technical sense, anticipating the more elaborated āsana of Patañjali a few centuries later.
11.prabhupada: To practice yoga, one should go to a secluded place and should lay kuśa grass on the ground and then cover it with a deerskin and a soft cloth. The seat should be neither too high nor too low and should be situated in a sacred place.
11.shankara: 6.11 In a clean place, having established a firm seat — not too high, not too low — covered with kusa grass, a deer-skin, and a cloth, in that order from below.
11.ramanuja: 6.11 In a clean place, having set up a steady seat for himself — neither too high nor too low — and having spread on it kuśa grass, a deer-skin and a cloth, in that order from below.

12.dev:
तत्रैकाग्रं मनः कृत्वा यतचित्तेन्द्रियक्रियः ।
उपविश्यासने युञ्ज्याद्योगमात्मविशुद्धये ॥ १२ ॥
12.iast:
tatraikāgraṁ manaḥ kṛtvā yata-cittendriya-kriyaḥ |
upaviśyāsane yuñjyād yogam ātma-viśuddhaye || 12 ||
12.literal: There, having made the mind one-pointed, with thought-and-sense-action controlled, sitting on the seat, one should yoke yoga for self-purification.
12.traditional: The instruction continues: having seated oneself on the prepared seat, with thought (citta) and sense-action (indriya-kriyā) controlled, with the mind made one-pointed (ekāgra), one should yoke yoga for self-purification (ātma-viśuddhi). The verse names the goal of this initial setup phase: not yet the highest samādhi, but ātma-viśuddhi, the cleansing of the self that prepares it for samādhi.
12.prabhupada: Thus making his life pure, and controlling his mind and senses, fixing the mind on one point, the yogī should practice yoga for the purification of the heart.
12.shankara: 6.12 There, having made the mind one-pointed, having controlled the thought-and-sense-activities, having sat down on the seat, let one practise yoga for the purification of the inner self.
12.ramanuja: 6.12 Sitting there, with the mind one-pointed, with the activities of thought and sense restrained, one should practise yoga for the purification of the self — for the removal of those vāsanas that obscure the Self.

13.dev:
समं कायशिरोग्रीवं धारयन्नचलं स्थिरः ।
सम्प्रेक्ष्य नासिकाग्रं स्वं दिशश्चानवलोकयन् ॥ १३ ॥
13.iast:
samaṁ kāya-śiro-grīvaṁ dhārayann acalaṁ sthiraḥ |
samprekṣya nāsikāgraṁ svaṁ diśaś cānavalokayan || 13 ||
13.literal: Holding body, head, and neck steady and motionless, gazing at the tip of one's own nose, not looking around in the directions.
13.traditional: The most physically specific verse in the Gita's meditation block. Body-head-neck steady and motionless; the gaze fixed at the tip of the nose; not looking around. This is technical yoga instruction, almost identical in spirit to instructions in later yogic literature. Shankara reads samprekṣya nāsikāgraṁ as 'as if gazing at the tip of the nose' — a focusing technique, not literal staring; Ramanuja reads it more literally.
13.prabhupada: One should hold one's body, neck and head erect in a straight line and stare steadily at the tip of the nose. Thus, with an unagitated, subdued mind, devoid of fear, completely free from sex life, one should meditate upon Me within the heart and make Me the ultimate goal of life.
13.shankara: 6.13 Holding the body, head, and neck even, steady and motionless; with the gaze as if directed to the tip of the nose, and not looking around in the directions.
13.ramanuja: 6.13 Holding the body, head and neck erect, immovable, looking at the tip of the nose without seeing in any direction.

14.dev:
प्रशान्तात्मा विगतभीर्ब्रह्मचारिव्रते स्थितः ।
मनः संयम्य मच्चित्तो युक्त आसीत मत्परः ॥ १४ ॥
14.iast:
praśāntātmā vigata-bhīr brahmacāri-vrate sthitaḥ |
manaḥ saṁyamya mac-citto yukta āsīta mat-paraḥ || 14 ||
14.literal: Calm of self, free from fear, established in the brahmacharya-vow, restraining the mind, with thought on me, yoked, let one sit, devoted to me.
14.traditional: The instruction's culminating phrase: with the inner conditions met (calm self, freedom from fear, the brahmacharya-vow), restraining the mind, with thought fixed on Krishna, yoked, devoted to Krishna — let the practitioner sit. The verse explicitly introduces the theistic dimension (mac-cittaḥ, mat-paraḥ) that becomes central in later chapters; in chapter 6 it functions as the focal object of meditation.
14.prabhupada: Thus, with an unagitated, subdued mind, devoid of fear, completely free from sex life, one should meditate upon Me within the heart and make Me the ultimate goal of life.
14.shankara: 6.14 With a calm self, fearless, established in the vow of celibacy, having restrained the mind, with the mind fixed on Me, yukta — engaged — let him sit, regarding Me as the supreme.
14.ramanuja: 6.14 With a tranquil self, fearless, firm in the vow of brahmacharya, having restrained the mind, with the mind fixed on Me, established in yoga, let him sit regarding Me as the highest object.

15.dev:
युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी नियतमानसः ।
शान्तिं निर्वाणपरमां मत्संस्थामधिगच्छति ॥ १५ ॥
15.iast:
yuñjann evaṁ sadātmānaṁ yogī niyata-mānasaḥ |
śāntiṁ nirvāṇa-paramāṁ mat-saṁsthām adhigacchati || 15 ||
15.literal: Yoking the self thus continually, the yogi of disciplined mind attains peace, with nirvāṇa as its highest end, abiding in me.
15.traditional: Krishna names the destination of the practice: śānti, peace, with nirvāṇa as its supreme term, abiding in him. The verse closes the somatic-instruction-and-internal-conditions block (6.10-6.14) by naming what the practitioner reaches.
15.prabhupada: Thus practicing constant control of the body, mind and activities, the mystic transcendentalist, his mind regulated, attains to the kingdom of God [or the abode of Kṛṣṇa] by cessation of material existence.
15.shankara: 6.15 The yogi engaging the mind in this way constantly, with the mind controlled, attains to the supreme peace which terminates in nirvāṇa, abiding in Me.
15.ramanuja: 6.15 The yogin who engages the mind on the Self thus constantly, of subdued mind, attains the peace abiding in Me, the supreme peace of which the consummation is liberation.

16.dev:
नात्यश्नतस्तु योगोऽस्ति न चैकान्तमनश्नतः ।
न चातिस्वप्नशीलस्य जाग्रतो नैव चार्जुन ॥ १६ ॥
16.iast:
nāty-aśnatas tu yogo 'sti na caikāntam anaśnataḥ |
na cāti-svapna-śīlasya jāgrato naiva cārjuna || 16 ||
16.literal: Yoga is not for the over-eater, nor for the one who eats nothing at all, nor for the one who sleeps too much, nor for the one who stays awake, O Arjuna.
16.traditional: Krishna gives the middle-path doctrine. Yoga is rejected at both extremes — too much food, too little food; too much sleep, too much wakefulness. The verse rejects ascetic excess as firmly as it rejects indulgence. Both Shankara and Ramanuja read this as a categorical rejection of extremism; the practice requires a sustained moderate baseline.
16.prabhupada: There is no possibility of one's becoming a yogī, O Arjuna, if one eats too much or eats too little, sleeps too much or does not sleep enough.
16.shankara: 6.16 Yoga is not for him who eats too much, nor for him who does not eat at all; nor for him who is much given to sleep, nor for him who keeps awake too much, O Arjuna.
16.ramanuja: 6.16 Yoga is not for him who eats too much, nor for him who eats too little; nor for him who sleeps too much, nor for him who is given to wakefulness in excess.
anvaya: of one who eats too much
of one who does not eat
of one given to too much sleep

17.dev:
युक्ताहारविहारस्य युक्तचेष्टस्य कर्मसु ।
युक्तस्वप्नावबोधस्य योगो भवति दुःखहा ॥ १७ ॥
17.iast:
yuktāhāra-vihārasya yukta-ceṣṭasya karmasu |
yukta-svapnāvabodhasya yogo bhavati duḥkha-hā || 17 ||
17.literal: For one who is disciplined in eating and recreation, disciplined in actions, disciplined in sleeping and waking — yoga becomes the destroyer of misery.
17.traditional: The complement to 6.16. Yoga becomes duḥkha-hā (the destroyer of misery) for one who is yukta — joined, properly proportioned — in food and recreation, in actions, in sleep and wakefulness. The verse names a comprehensive moderation: not just diet but the full daily rhythm. The middle-path doctrine is operational, not merely abstemious.
17.prabhupada: He who is regulated in his habits of eating, sleeping, recreation and work can mitigate all material pains by practicing the yoga system.
17.shankara: 6.17 For him whose food and recreation are properly regulated, whose effort in action is regulated, whose sleep and waking are regulated, yoga becomes the destroyer of pain.
17.ramanuja: 6.17 For him who is moderate in food and recreation, who is moderate in his exertions in action, and who is moderate in sleep and waking, yoga becomes the destroyer of suffering.
anvaya: of one disciplined in eating and recreation
of one disciplined in sleep and waking

18.dev:
यदा विनियतं चित्तमात्मन्येवावतिष्ठते ।
निःस्पृहः सर्वकामेभ्यो युक्त इत्युच्यते तदा ॥ १८ ॥
18.iast:
yadā viniyataṁ cittam ātmany evāvatiṣṭhate |
niḥspṛhaḥ sarva-kāmebhyo yukta ity ucyate tadā || 18 ||
18.literal: When the disciplined thought is established only in the self, free from longing toward all desires — then one is said to be yukta.
18.traditional: Krishna defines yukta — the established condition. The disciplined mind, when it rests only in the self (ātmani eva avatiṣṭhate), free from longing for any desires, is yukta. The verse closes the discipline-instruction block (6.10-6.17) by naming the inner state that the discipline produces.
18.prabhupada: When the yogī, by practice of yoga, disciplines his mental activities and becomes situated in transcendence — devoid of all material desires — he is said to be well established in yoga.
18.shankara: 6.18 When the disciplined mind comes to rest in the Self alone, free from longing for all desires, then one is said to be yukta, joined in yoga.
18.ramanuja: 6.18 When the well-controlled mind rests only on the Self, free from all longing for any object of desire, then one is said to be 'yukta' — in union.

19.dev:
यथा दीपो निवातस्थो नेङ्गते सोपमा स्मृता ।
योगिनो यतचित्तस्य युञ्जतो योगमात्मनः ॥ १९ ॥
19.iast:
yathā dīpo nivātastho neṅgate sopamā smṛtā |
yogino yata-cittasya yuñjato yogam ātmanaḥ || 19 ||
19.literal: As a lamp set in a place free of wind does not flicker — that simile is remembered for the yogi of controlled thought yoking yoga of the self.
19.traditional: The lamp simile, the most famous image in chapter 6 and one of the most famous in the entire Gita. The yogi of controlled thought, yoked in self-yoga, is like a lamp in a windless place: it does not flicker. The image is precise — not 'a lamp' generally, but a lamp specifically protected from wind. The protection is the discipline; the steadiness is the yoga; the flame is the attention.
19.prabhupada: As a lamp in a windless place does not waver, so the transcendentalist, whose mind is controlled, remains always steady in his meditation on the transcendent self.
19.shankara: 6.19 As a lamp placed in a windless spot does not flicker, that is the simile remembered for the yogi of controlled mind, who is engaged in yoga of the Self.
19.ramanuja: 6.19 As a lamp placed in a sheltered spot, where there is no wind, does not flicker — even so is the simile recorded for the steady mind of the yogi engaged in concentration on the Self.

20.dev:
यत्रोपरमते चित्तं निरुद्धं योगसेवया ।
यत्र चैवात्मनात्मानं पश्यन्नात्मनि तुष्यति ॥ २० ॥
20.iast:
yatroparamate cittaṁ niruddhaṁ yoga-sevayā |
yatra caivātmanātmānaṁ paśyann ātmani tuṣyati || 20 ||
20.literal: Where the thought, restrained by the practice of yoga, comes to rest; where seeing the self by the self, one is satisfied in the self.
20.traditional: The first of four verses describing the samādhi-state. The state where thought (citta) restrained by yoga-practice comes to rest; where the practitioner sees the self by the self and is satisfied (tuṣyati) in the self. Shankara reads this as the Advaita realization: the seer, seeing, and seen collapse. Ramanuja reads it as the yogi seeing his own self by his own discriminating intellect, satisfied in that vision.
20.prabhupada: In the stage of perfection called trance, or samādhi, one's mind is completely restrained from material mental activities by practice of yoga. This perfection is characterized by one's ability to see the Self by the pure mind and to relish and rejoice in the Self.
20.shankara: 6.20 Where the mind, restrained by the practice of yoga, becomes still; where seeing the Self by the self the yogi rejoices in the Self.
20.ramanuja: 6.20 The state in which the mind, restrained by the practice of yoga, attains rest; in which, seeing the Self by the discriminating self, one delights in the Self.

21.dev:
सुखमात्यन्तिकं यत्तद्बुद्धिग्राह्यमतीन्द्रियम् ।
वेत्ति यत्र न चैवायं स्थितश्चलति तत्त्वतः ॥ २१ ॥
21.iast:
sukham ātyantikaṁ yat tad buddhi-grāhyam atīndriyam |
vetti yatra na caivāyaṁ sthitaś calati tattvataḥ || 21 ||
21.literal: Where one knows that boundless happiness which is grasped by intellect and beyond the senses; established there, one does not waver from the truth.
21.traditional: The samādhi-description continues. The state where the practitioner knows boundless happiness (sukham ātyantikam), graspable by intellect (buddhi-grāhya), beyond the senses (atīndriya); having reached this state, one does not waver from the truth. Both Shankara and Ramanuja read this as the description of the experiential reality of samādhi: the happiness is real, accessible to the discriminating intellect, but not via the sense-organs.
21.prabhupada: In that joyous state, one is situated in boundless transcendental happiness, realized through transcendental senses. Established thus, one never departs from the truth.
21.shankara: 6.21 Where one knows that infinite joy which is to be apprehended by the intellect and which transcends the senses; established there one swerves not from the truth.
21.ramanuja: 6.21 The state in which one knows that infinite joy which is to be grasped by the intellect and which transcends the senses; in which the yogi, established, does not stir from the truth.

22.dev:
यं लब्ध्वा चापरं लाभं मन्यते नाधिकं ततः ।
यस्मिन्स्थितो न दुःखेन गुरुणापि विचाल्यते ॥ २२ ॥
22.iast:
yaṁ labdhvā cāparaṁ lābhaṁ manyate nādhikaṁ tataḥ |
yasmin sthito na duḥkhena guruṇāpi vicālyate || 22 ||
22.literal: Which having gained, one thinks no other gain greater than that; established in which, one is not shaken even by heavy sorrow.
22.traditional: Krishna names two further marks of samādhi: it is the gain such that no other gain seems greater; established in it, one is not shaken even by guruṇā duḥkhena (heavy sorrow). The verse is precise: not 'no sorrow comes,' but 'sorrow does not shake the established state.' The state is robust, not insulated.
22.prabhupada: Established thus, one never departs from the truth, and upon gaining this he thinks there is no greater gain. Being situated in such a position, one is never shaken, even in the midst of greatest difficulty.
22.shankara: 6.22 Which having gained, no other gain is reckoned higher than it; established in which, one is not shaken even by heavy sorrow.
22.ramanuja: 6.22 Which, having gained, the yogi considers no other gain superior; established in which, he is not shaken even by heaviest grief.

23.dev:
तं विद्याद्दुःखसंयोगवियोगं योगसंज्ञितम् ।
स निश्चयेन योक्तव्यो योगोऽनिर्विण्णचेतसा ॥ २३ ॥
23.iast:
taṁ vidyād duḥkha-saṁyoga-viyogaṁ yoga-saṁjñitam |
sa niścayena yoktavyo yogo 'nirviṇṇa-cetasā || 23 ||
23.literal: Know that severance of union with sorrow to be what is called yoga. That yoga is to be practised with determination and an unwearied mind.
23.traditional: Krishna defines yoga in this context as duḥkha-saṁyoga-viyoga — the severance of union with sorrow. The four-verse samādhi-description (6.20-6.23) closes with a call to action: this state is reachable, and it is to be practiced with niścaya (determination) and anirviṇṇa-cetas (unwearied mind). The verse pivots from describing samādhi to enjoining its sustained pursuit.
23.prabhupada: Let it be known that the severance of all contact with pain is called yoga. This yoga should be practiced with determination and an undismayed mind.
23.shankara: 6.23 Let one know that disunion from all union with sorrow to be what is called yoga. That yoga should be practised with determination, with an unwearied mind.
23.ramanuja: 6.23 Let it be known that the severance of contact with pain is what is called yoga. This yoga is to be practised with firm resolve and with a mind not despondent.

24.iast:
saṅkalpa-prabhavān kāmāṁs tyaktvā sarvān aśeṣataḥ |
manasaivendriya-grāmaṁ viniyamya samantataḥ || 24 ||
24.literal: Abandoning entirely all desires born of fancy (saṅkalpa), restraining the host of senses on every side by the mind alone.
24.traditional: Krishna gives the practice-instruction proper. Abandon (tyaktvā) entirely (aśeṣataḥ) all desires born of saṅkalpa-imaginings; by the mind alone (manasā eva), restrain (viniyamya) the host of senses (indriya-grāma) on every side (samantataḥ). The verse names two simultaneous moves: outward, the abandonment of desires-from-fancies; inward, the restraint of senses by mind. Note: the construction continues into 6.25 ('gradually arrest…'). 6.24-6.25 form a single sentence in Sanskrit; some editions print them together.
24.prabhupada: One should engage oneself in the practice of yoga with determination and faith and not be deviated from the path. One should abandon, without exception, all material desires born of mental speculation and thus control all the senses on all sides by the mind.
24.shankara: 6.24 Abandoning, without reserve, all desires that arise from fancy (sankalpa); restraining all the senses by the mind alone, on every side.
24.ramanuja: 6.24 Renouncing entirely all desires born of imaginings, and restraining by the mind itself the entire group of senses from all directions.
